acholuria: A- 'without' plus chol- 'bile' plus -uria 'in the urine.' Eighteenth-century clinicians already knew that a jaundiced patient with pale urine had a fundamentally different disease from one with tea-brown urine.
anuria: Simply the alpha-privative on the Greek for urine.
balanitis: The Greek anatomists thought the tip of the male organ looked like an acorn.
bordetella: Named for the Belgian bacteriologist Jules Bordet. His Nobel came for antibody complement, not for the cough he cultured.
bronchiectasis: One radiographic hallmark of this condition is the 'signet ring' cross-section, where the dilated airway exceeds the diameter of its companion pulmonary artery; Laennec first described the parenchymal dilations at autopsy in 1819.
burns: Ambroise Paré, the sixteenth-century French army surgeon, replaced the hot-oil treatment with a gentler poultice and changed the field forever.
cachexia: One 2011 international consensus definition of this syndrome by Fearon and colleagues requires greater than five percent weight loss over six months plus low BMI or sarcopenia, driven by tumor necrosis factor-alpha and interleukin-6.
catatonia: One classic sign of this state is waxy flexibility, in which the patient holds an examiner-imposed posture indefinitely; Karl Kahlbaum coined the term in 1874 for what he called 'Spannungsirresein.'
cheilosis: Persian and Byzantine physicians described the fissuring at the corners of the mouth that comes with a particular deficiency; the Greek word for lip supplies the stem.
choledocholithiasis: One classical Charcot triad for the acute cholangitis complicating this condition — right-upper-quadrant pain, jaundice, and fever — expands to Reynolds pentad with the addition of shock and altered mentation.
cholecystectomy: The first was performed by Carl Langenbuch in Berlin in 1882. The laparoscopic version came a century later.
choroiditis: First cleanly seen with the ophthalmoscope Helmholtz invented in 1851.
colitis: One 1859 Samuel Wilks description at Guy's Hospital in a patient of Sir William Jenner distinguished the idiopathic ulcerative form of this condition from bacillary dysentery, which killed more American Civil War soldiers than the rifles.
covid: One clinical feature that distinguished the early strain of this respiratory illness was ageusia and anosmia; its Wuhan sequence was deposited on Virological.org in January 2020 by Zhang and colleagues.
crigler: Two American pediatricians described the enzyme deficiency in 1952 — a rare hereditary jaundice.
dacryoadenitis: Greek dakryon 'tear' plus adeno 'gland' — the paired gland just above the outer eye that makes them.
dislocation: Hippocrates wrote three treatises on it. His method for reducing the shoulder — foot in the axilla, pull — still bears his name.
dissociation: Pierre Janet's late-nineteenth-century Paris lectures separated this psychiatric umbrella term from what Freud would soon rebrand as repression.
dupuytrens: Napoleon's chief surgeon lent his name to the palmar contracture that gradually curls the ring and little digits inward.
dysgeusia: From Greek dys- 'bad' plus geusis 'taste.' A distorted flavor from chemotherapy or zinc deficiency.
dysmenorrhea: Greek dys- plus menorrhea 'monthly flow.' Prostaglandins peak in the first two days; NSAIDs quiet them.
dysuria: One classical bedside evaluation of this symptom in an otherwise-healthy woman is a leukocyte-esterase-positive urine dipstick, which supports the three-day empiric nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfa the Choosing Wisely campaign endorses.
ecchymosis: The Greek ek- 'out' plus chymos 'juice.' The color chart of a bruise, in one word.
ecthyma: Greek 'to erupt.' A deeper, punched-out version of an ordinary impetigo.
epiglottitis: One pathognomonic lateral neck radiograph in this pediatric emergency shows the 'thumb sign,' and its incidence collapsed after routine H. influenzae type B conjugate vaccination in the 1990s.
erysipelas: The Greek word for 'red skin.' Hippocrates used it. Fehleisen isolated the streptococcus in 1883.
erythema: From Greek erythros 'red.' The stem hides inside the names of many rashes.
euvolemia: One classical bedside assessment of this state uses the jugular venous pressure at 45 degrees, mucous-membrane moisture, and orthostatic vital signs; it guides the crystalloid rate the ICU nurse should hang.
fibrosis: The Latin fibra, 'thread.' What replaces working tissue when the body gives up on healing.
fungemia: The rise of ICU-level central lines in the 1970s made this a household word in hospital microbiology.
gliadin: Isolated from wheat by the Italian chemist Beccari in 1728 — two centuries before it was linked to a small-bowel disease.
hordeolum: One clinical clue distinguishing this eyelid inflammation from a chalazion is its pain and rapid onset at a lash-line follicle; the Latin name is the same word Pliny used for a barley grain.
hyperaldosteronism: An overshooting mineralocorticoid. The eponym belongs to the American endocrinologist who described the primary adrenal form in 1955.
kwashiorkor: Cicely Williams, a Jamaican-born pediatrician on the Gold Coast in the 1930s, adopted the local Ga name for the syndrome she was writing up in the Lancet.
laryngomalacia: From the Greek for 'softening.' The most common cause of noisy breathing in babies; the cartilage stiffens on its own by the second birthday.
lassa: In 1969 a Nigerian mission nurse fell ill; two Yale researchers who tried to isolate the virus died before it was named for her town.
lymphedema: Milroy described the hereditary form in 1892; the sporadic post-surgical form is far more common today.
melanoma: From Greek melas 'black.' The one skin cancer that has killed nobles since the Renaissance.
molluscum: The Latin diminutive of mollis, 'soft.' Small pearls with a dimple; children swap them at the pool.
mucocele: Greek mucus plus kele 'tumor.' The blue-tinged blister after a minor lip bite.
muscular: The Latin diminutive of mus, 'a little mouse' — because the twitching bicep looked like one darting under the skin.
nosocomial: Semmelweis in Vienna published his data on it in 1861. He was ignored, and died in an asylum four years later.
oligodendroglioma: Bailey and Cushing named it in the 1920s for a cell type the Spanish neuroanatomist Río Hortega had just described.
orthopnea: One bedside indicator of this symptom is the number of pillows a patient stacks to sleep; venous return to a failing left ventricle increases in the supine position, worsening the pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure.
parkinsonism: The 'Essay on the Shaking Palsy' was published in London in 1817 by the physician whose name now covers the whole syndrome.
peliosis: Wenzel illustrated the blood-filled hepatic cavities in 1861. They returned to the literature in the 1980s when bodybuilders and AIDS patients began dying with them.
periodontitis: The Egyptians treated it with honey and incense. The teeth are still there; what anchors them is not.
petechiae: One test for this cutaneous finding uses a glass tumbler pressed against the skin, and its meningococcal association makes a non-blanching rash in a febrile child an infectious-disease emergency.
phthisis: Hippocrates named it 'wasting.' The bacillus behind it was not identified until Koch's 1882 lecture at the Berlin Physiological Society.
plague: Rodents, fleas, buboes, and a nursery rhyme.
polyneuritis: Greek poly- 'many' plus neur- 'nerve' plus -itis 'inflammation.' Bilateral, symmetric, and ascending.
polyneuropathy: The 'stocking-and-glove' distribution has been recognized since the nineteenth century as the calling card of diabetes, alcohol, and lead.
pyoderma: Greek pyon 'pus' plus derma 'skin.' Also names a striking ulcerative autoimmune disorder that responds to steroids, not antibiotics.
rickets: One classical bony finding in this pediatric deficiency state is the 'rachitic rosary' of costochondral beading; Francis Glisson wrote the first monograph on it in 1650, and cod-liver-oil trials in early-twentieth-century Vienna proved out the antirachitic factor.
sarcopenia: The word was coined only in 1988 by an American nutrition researcher, from the Greek for 'poverty of flesh.'
sialadenitis: One vaccine-preventable childhood infection of this glandular tissue produces a soft parotid swelling that displaces the earlobe upward and outward; Enders and colleagues cultured the paramyxovirus in 1945.
strangulated: The adjective from the Latin for choking. Turns an ordinary hernia from urgent to emergent.
takayasu: The Japanese ophthalmologist Mikito Takayasu described the arteritis in 1908 by noticing wreaths of new vessels around the optic nerves of his young female patients.
takotsubo: A Japanese pot for catching octopus. Grief kills.
thromboembolism: Virchow's 1850s triad — stasis, endothelial injury, hypercoagulability — was proposed to explain how a clot in one vein could kill a patient elsewhere.
tracheomalacia: From Greek malakia, 'softness.' The cartilaginous rings that hold the airway open are too pliant.
ureteric: The Greeks named the ureter from the verb 'to make water.' The adjective often carries 'stone' or 'reflux' behind it.
vaginitis: One Amsel criteria set for the bacterial-overgrowth form of this inflammation requires three of four findings including thin gray discharge, clue cells on wet mount, pH above 4.5, and a positive whiff test with KOH.
varicose: Roman legionaries had them burnt out with a hot iron. Aulus Cornelius Celsus described the surgery in the first century CE.
varicose veins: One classical treatment for this condition, described in the first-century De Medicina of Aulus Cornelius Celsus, involved a hot-iron cauterization of the failed valves in the great saphenous.
volvulus: From the Latin volvere, 'to turn.' On a plain abdominal film it draws the shape of a coffee bean.
vulvovaginitis: In the eighteenth century it went by 'the whites.' Estrogen deficiency underlies most modern cases.
whipple: The American surgeon lent his name to a pancreatic-head resection in 1935; separately, in 1907, he had described a bacterial malabsorption disease he found at autopsy.
whooping: The inspiratory sound is the reason for the vernacular English name. The P in DTaP was added to prevent it.
liver failure: One classical triad defining the acute form of this state, from Trey and Davidson's 1970 monograph, requires encephalopathy plus coagulopathy plus the absence of prior chronic disease; King's College criteria guide transplant listing.
herpes simplex: One mucocutaneous finding of this infection is the Tzanck smear's multinucleated giant cells; Weller and Enders isolated the causative alphaherpesvirus in 1949 by serial passage in human amnion.
nephrolithiasis: One acoustic technique developed by Dornier engineers in the 1980s fragments this condition's structures using focused shock waves generated outside the body, replacing the open-flank surgery Hippocrates already knew.
alzheimers: One neuropathological hallmark of this condition is Divry's Congo-red-birefringent extracellular plaque of beta-amyloid, first illustrated in the Tübingen presentation of Auguste Deter's brain in 1906.
urinary tract infection: One classical urinalysis finding in this condition, first described by Kass in the 1950s, is a colony-forming-unit count of at least 100,000 per milliliter from a clean-catch midstream specimen.
carbuncle: One dermatologic feature of this deep pyoderma is a coalesced cluster of follicular abscesses with multiple sinus tracts; its Latin diminutive is the same word Pliny used for a small ember.
borderline personality disorder: One 1938 paper by Adolph Stern first placed this psychiatric entity at what he called the border of neurosis and psychosis; Kernberg's object-relations framing dominated the mid-century American nosology.
glomerulonephritis: One nineteenth-century description of this renal condition came from Richard Bright at Guy's Hospital, who linked the dropsical face and dark urine to inflamed glomeruli at autopsy in 1836.
small bowel obstruction: One characteristic plain-film finding of this condition is the stack of dilated air-fluid levels stepping across the abdomen; postoperative adhesions and incarcerated hernias lead the etiologies in developed countries.
dementia: One diagnostic subclassification of this cognitive condition draws on the DSM-5 major-versus-mild framing; Philippe Pinel's 1801 nosography first separated its chronic loss of reasoning from acute delirium.
thalassemia: One geographically named subclass of this hemoglobin disorder was described by Detroit pediatrician Thomas Cooley in 1925; the term borrows the Greek word for the Mediterranean sea it clusters around.
tuberculosis: One radiographic finding of this condition is the Ghon complex — a calcified parenchymal focus plus a hilar node — and Koch demonstrated the causative bacillus on Lowenstein-Jensen medium after his 1882 Berlin lecture.
duodenal ulcer: One 1982 experiment self-administered by Barry Marshall proved that a spiral gram-negative organism, not stress or spice, drives this recurrent lesion at the pylorus; he shared the 2005 Nobel with Robin Warren.
proteinuria: One nineteenth-century test for this urinary finding, from Bright's Guy's Hospital work, heated the specimen over a candle to precipitate the tell-tale white cloud of glomerular leak.
pcos: One 1935 case series in the American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology by Stein and Leventhal described this syndrome in seven women with enlarged, cystic ovaries, amenorrhea, and hirsutism.
kawasaki: One serious sequela of this pediatric febrile vasculitis is the coronary artery aneurysm; the Tokyo Red Cross pediatrician who described it in 1967 initially considered it a benign self-limited exanthem.
prediabetes: One diagnostic threshold for this metabolic state was set at a fasting plasma glucose of 100 by the American Diabetes Association in 2003; the International Expert Committee later added a hemoglobin A1c of 5.7 to 6.4 percent.
menorrhagia: One first-line non-hormonal therapy for this gynecologic symptom is the antifibrinolytic tranexamic acid, which the NICE guidelines added after RCTs by Lethaby and colleagues.
lymphoma: One 1832 monograph by a Guy's Hospital physician described the painless nodal enlargement and splenomegaly that would later carry his name; the Reed-Sternberg cell would not be described for another sixty years.
narcolepsy: One 2000 CSF finding by Nishino and Mignot in patients with this sleep disorder was near-zero levels of the lateral hypothalamic peptide orexin, also known as hypocretin.
supraventricular tachycardia: One first-line pharmacologic treatment for this narrow-complex arrhythmia is a rapid IV push of adenosine, which transiently blocks AV nodal conduction via A1 receptors on cardiomyocytes.
constipation: One 2016 Rome IV committee formalized this symptom's diagnostic criteria to include Bristol Stool Scale types 1-2, straining, and infrequent evacuation, requiring at least three months of the pattern.
retinoblastoma: One 1971 Alfred Knudson analysis of this pediatric ocular malignancy proposed the two-hit hypothesis, using the RB1 tumor-suppressor locus on chromosome 13q14 as the paradigm.
gerd: One bedside test for this condition, described by Bernstein and Baker in 1958, instills 0.1-normal HCl into the distal esophagus through a nasal catheter and asks the patient to report the reproduction of symptoms.
tachycardia: One 1846 Ludwig kymograph tracing first captured this state as a beat frequency above 100; Willem Einthoven's 1903 string galvanometer moved its documentation from the pulse to the ECG.
pleural effusion: One 1972 rule by Richard Light distinguishes this collection's exudative from transudative etiologies using a fluid-to-serum protein ratio above 0.5 or an LDH ratio above 0.6.
kidney stones: One recurrence-prevention regimen for this condition employs potassium citrate to raise urinary pH and citrate excretion; recorded in Egyptian mummies, its lithotripsy dates to the 1980s Dornier device.
tennis elbow: One clinical maneuver for this condition, Cozen's test, reproduces the patient's pain when the examiner resists wrist extension with the elbow held at 90 degrees; the tendon involved is the extensor carpi radialis brevis.
neuroblastoma: One catecholamine breakdown product elevated in the urine of children with this sympathetic-chain malignancy is vanillylmandelic acid; Wright named the tumor in a 1910 Journal of Experimental Medicine paper.
cirrhosis: One 1963 Child-Pugh scoring system for this chronic hepatic condition sums bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, and encephalopathy grade to stratify surgical risk; Laennec named it in 1819 for the tawny color at autopsy.
chest pain: One 1996 HEART score by Six and colleagues from the Netherlands stratifies this presenting symptom's 30-day major-adverse-event risk using history, ECG, age, risk factors, and troponin.
endometriosis: One 1927 John Sampson proposal for the pathogenesis of this condition invokes retrograde menstrual reflux implanting viable stromal cells on peritoneal surfaces; laparoscopy remains diagnostic a century later.
pneumothorax: One classical physical-exam finding in the tension variant of this condition is tracheal deviation away from the hyperresonant hemithorax; needle decompression at the second intercostal space, midclavicular line, is the emergency maneuver.
cholera: One 1854 map by John Snow plotting this diarrheal illness around the Broad Street pump in Soho is credited as the founding artifact of modern epidemiology.
bradycardia: One 1988 revised Bethesda classification of this rhythm state defines it as a sinus rate below 60 beats per minute in adults; the athlete's heart and beta-blocker use are its most common benign causes.
low back pain: One 1996 Waddell's-signs framework for this presenting complaint identifies five behavioral markers — superficial tenderness, simulation, distraction, regional weakness, and overreaction — that suggest non-organic contribution.
addisons: One 1855 description by Thomas Addison of Guy's Hospital catalogued this endocrine condition's bronzed skin, weight loss, and salt craving in eleven patients with adrenal cortical destruction.
esophageal varices: One 1988 Sarin classification stratifies this vascular finding by size and location within the food-tube; balloon tamponade with the Sengstaken-Blakemore tube buys time before endoscopic band ligation.
dysphagia: One 1918 Barclay classification of this symptom distinguished the oropharyngeal from the esophageal phase; video-fluoroscopic swallow studies now supplant the barium swallow for the pharyngeal phase.
deep vein thrombosis: One 1856 Rudolf Virchow triad — venous stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability — explained this venous condition's pathogenesis; Wells scoring stratifies pre-test probability before D-dimer or duplex ultrasound.
aortic aneurysm: One 1557 Andreas Vesalius illustration in De Humani Corporis Fabrica depicted a saccular version of this vascular finding at autopsy; Dubost performed the first successful resection with graft in 1951.
pharyngitis: One 2012 Centor score for this common symptom uses age, tonsillar exudate, tender anterior cervical adenopathy, absence of cough, and fever to stratify the pre-test probability of Streptococcus pyogenes.
emphysema: One 1963 Charles Fletcher paper in the British Medical Journal traced this parenchymal disease's decline in FEV1 across smokers versus non-smokers, establishing the accelerated-loss trajectory in susceptible smokers.
aids: One June 1981 MMWR report by the CDC noted five Los Angeles cases of Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia in previously healthy homosexual men, launching the recognition of this immunodeficiency syndrome.
sunburn: One 1928 Karl Hausser identification of ultraviolet-B as the erythemogenic wavelength band for this cutaneous injury led to the modern SPF classification system introduced by Franz Greiter in 1962.
migraine: One 1988 International Headache Society classification requires four hours of unilateral pulsatile pain plus nausea or photophobia for this neurologic condition; Hippocrates called it hemikrania.
jaundice: One 1846 Charles Bunsen spectroscopic analysis of a yellow bile pigment first identified this condition's biochemical basis; Van den Bergh's 1913 diazo reaction later distinguished conjugated from unconjugated fractions.
copd: One 1965 term coined by Sir William Briscoe encompasses this obstructive respiratory syndrome; the GOLD initiative categorizes severity by post-bronchodilator FEV1 percent-predicted.
urticaria: One classical wheal-and-flare morphology of this condition mimics a brush against Urtica dioica, the stinging nettle for which it is named; H1-blockade with second-generation cetirizine or fexofenadine is first-line.
diabetes mellitus: One 1921 Toronto experiment by Frederick Banting and Charles Best used pancreatic-duct-ligated dog extract to reverse the emaciation of this metabolic condition, earning the 1923 Nobel with Macleod.
atherosclerosis: One 1913 Nikolai Anichkov rabbit experiment established the cholesterol-fed model of this arterial condition; the accumulating porridge-like intimal plaques give the disease its Greek athere-derived name.
whooping cough: One 1906 culture of the causative gram-negative coccobacillus for this respiratory illness by Jules Bordet and Octave Gengou used a potato-blood-glycerol medium; the P in the DTaP vaccine curbs it today.
hearing loss: One 2001 Weber and Rinne tuning-fork combination distinguishes this symptom's conductive and sensorineural etiologies at the bedside; presbycusis dominates the age-adjusted prevalence data.
hypocalcemia: One classical Chvostek sign for this electrolyte disturbance twitches the ipsilateral facial muscles when the examiner taps the parotid; Trousseau's variant inflates a blood-pressure cuff to elicit carpopedal spasm.
diarrhea: One 2006 introduction of the pentavalent RotaTeq vaccine by Kapikian, Wyatt, and colleagues dropped worldwide childhood mortality from this symptom by nearly half a million per year.
hemorrhoids: One 1975 Milligan-Morgan open hemorrhoidectomy remains a standard surgical option for this Grade III or IV condition; the Greek etymology reads as 'blood flow.'
retinal detachment: One 1929 Jules Gonin pathogenesis paper for this ophthalmologic emergency identified the horseshoe tear as the initiating lesion, guiding the modern pars-plana vitrectomy and scleral-buckling repairs.
trigeminal neuralgia: One 1773 John Fothergill description of this shock-like facial pain in twenty-eight patients coined the modern nosologic entity, formerly known as 'tic douloureux' in nineteenth-century Parisian neurology.
hyperkalemia: One serial ECG progression in this electrolyte disturbance runs from peaked T waves through PR prolongation to sine-wave pattern; calcium gluconate stabilizes myocyte membranes while insulin-and-glucose drives the shift.
cystitis: One classical dysuria-frequency-urgency triad of this lower-tract infection led Edward Kass in the 1950s to define significant bacteriuria at 100,000 CFU per milliliter of clean-catch midstream urine.
bacteremia: One 1992 ACCP/SCCM consensus definition for this hematogenous infection distinguished it from sepsis by the absence of systemic inflammatory response criteria; the modern qSOFA replaces SIRS in the Sepsis-3 update.
hidradenitis: One 1854 Aristide Verneuil description of this apocrine-follicle-blocking suppurative disease in the axilla and groin gave it the eponym; Hurley staging guides surgical versus medical treatment.
als: One 1874 Jean-Martin Charcot description in the Comptes Rendus of this motor-neuron disease distinguished it from progressive muscular atrophy by involvement of both upper and lower motor tracts; Lou Gehrig's 1939 farewell brought it into American consciousness.
obesity: One 1972 Ancel Keys paper standardized the Quetelet index as this condition's screening tool under the modern acronym; the WHO declared its epidemic status in the 1997 Geneva consultation.
uveitis: One HLA-B27-linked anterior form of this ocular inflammation associates with ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease; Behçet's, sarcoidosis, and toxoplasmosis complete the classical differential.
chlamydia: One 1907 Halberstaedter and von Prowazek discovery of inclusion bodies from a trachoma scraping first identified this obligate-intracellular pathogen; nucleic-acid amplification testing on first-catch urine now supplants McCoy-cell culture.
uterine cancer: One 1988 FIGO staging revision for this endometrioid malignancy incorporated depth of myometrial invasion and cervical involvement; unopposed estrogen and Lynch syndrome are the major genetic risk factors.
brain tumor: One 2016 WHO CNS classification for this intracranial-mass category incorporated molecular markers alongside histology, using IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion to divide the diffuse gliomas.
anemia: One 1926 George Whipple, George Minot, and William Murphy Nobel-winning liver-feeding experiments for this hematologic condition led to the isolation of cyanocobalamin two decades later.
fatigue: One 1994 Fukuda case-definition for the chronic form of this symptom required at least six months of debilitating exhaustion plus four of eight cognitive and somatic criteria; the term myalgic encephalomyelitis is now often preferred.
ascites: One 1988 SAAG framework by Runyon separates the etiologies of this peritoneal fluid collection at a serum-to-ascites albumin gradient of 1.1 g/dL, distinguishing portal-hypertensive from non-portal causes.
ibd: One 1932 Burrill Crohn, Ginzburg, and Oppenheimer paper in JAMA described the regional ileal form of this chronic inflammatory syndrome; Wilks and Moxon's ulcerative variant predates them by seventy years.
hernia: One 1887 Edoardo Bassini repair of the inguinal form of this fascial defect suture-approximated the transversalis fascia to the shelving edge of the inguinal ligament; the tension-free Lichtenstein mesh repair supplanted it in the 1980s.
graves disease: One 1835 Dublin lecture at the Meath Hospital by Robert James Graves described this endocrine condition's triad of goiter, palpitations, and exophthalmos; the thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin behind it was not identified until 1956.
ileus: One 1899 William Osler chapter distinguished this postoperative gut paralysis from mechanical obstruction; the neurogenic and inflammatory theories now share the modern pathophysiological understanding with opioid-mu-receptor effects.
hematochezia: One classical distinction between this symptom and melena hinges on transit time and source location above or below the ileocecal valve; radionuclide-labeled RBC scanning localizes intermittent brisk bleeds.
alport syndrome: One 1927 A. Cecil Alport observation in a British family series linked this heritable disease's progressive glomerulonephritis with sensorineural hearing loss and lenticonus; the underlying defect is in a collagen IV alpha chain.
goodpasture syndrome: One 1919 Ernest Goodpasture autopsy of a young man dying after influenza described this autoimmune syndrome; the responsible antibody targets the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen in the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes.
danon disease: One 1981 Moris Danon description in Neurology of this X-linked lysosomal storage disorder found intracytoplasmic vacuoles containing autophagic material; the LAMP2 gene mutation drives the hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, skeletal myopathy, and cognitive impairment triad.
amyloidosis: One Congo-red-birefringent staining pattern under polarized light identifies the beta-pleated-sheet deposits of this systemic condition; the light-chain (AL), transthyretin (ATTR), and serum amyloid A (AA) subtypes each dictate different organ tropism.
idiopathic intracranial hypertension: One diagnostic criterion for this syndrome, the modified Dandy criteria updated by Friedman in 2013, requires elevated opening pressure above 25 cm water on lumbar puncture in the absence of a mass; young obese women bear the disproportionate burden.
eosinophilic esophagitis: One 2011 consensus recommendation for this chronic Th2-driven mucosal disease requires at least 15 eosinophils per high-power field on esophageal biopsy after eight weeks of PPI; food impaction in young men is the classical presentation.
castleman disease: One 1956 Benjamin Castleman description of a mediastinal lymph node lesion resembling a thymoma launched the recognition of this hyperplastic hematologic disorder; HHV-8-associated multicentric variants dominate in HIV-positive patients.
scleroderma: One 1980 ACR classification split this connective-tissue disease into limited (CREST) and diffuse cutaneous variants by the extent of skin thickening; anti-Scl-70 and anti-centromere antibodies further prognosticate pulmonary versus vascular complications.
guillain barre: One 1916 Guillain, Barré, and Strohl description of this ascending flaccid paralysis noted the albuminocytological dissociation on CSF sampling; Campylobacter jejuni preceding infection is the leading trigger for the axonal variants.
erythema nodosum: One classical panniculitis of the pretibial subcutaneous fat presents as tender red nodules; sarcoidosis, streptococcal pharyngitis, IBD, and coccidioidomycosis lead its differential diagnosis of triggers.
antiphospholipid syndrome: One 2006 Sydney criteria for this hypercoagulable disorder require both clinical (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity) and laboratory (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, or anti-β2-glycoprotein-I) findings on two occasions ≥12 weeks apart; Hughes' 1983 lecture put it on the map.
thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura: One 1924 Eli Moschcowitz description in a 16-year-old girl outlined this microangiopathic hemolytic disease's classic pentad — thrombocytopenia, MAHA, fever, renal impairment, and neurologic dysfunction; ADAMTS13 deficiency below 10% is the defining lesion.
immune thrombocytopenic purpura: One 1735 Paul Gottlieb Werlhof description of 'morbus maculosus haemorrhagicus' foreshadowed this autoimmune bleeding disorder; anti-GPIIb/IIIa antibodies opsonize platelets for splenic destruction.
sarcoidosis: One 1889 Ernest Besnier lupus-pernio description and Boeck's 1899 'multiple benign sarkoid' framed this multisystem granulomatous disease; noncaseating epithelioid granulomas plus bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy are the hallmark radiographic and histologic pair.
cholecystitis: One Murphy's-sign inspiratory arrest on right-upper-quadrant palpation supports the acute form of this condition; HIDA scan showing non-visualization of the organ within 60 minutes clinches it when ultrasound is equivocal.
temporal arteritis: One 1932 Bayard T. Horton report at the Mayo Clinic described the temporal artery biopsy findings in this large-vessel vasculitis; jaw claudication and PMR overlap are the classical prompts to steroid initiation before biopsy to prevent vision loss.
pemphigus vulgaris: One Nikolsky-sign lateral shear of the skin producing intraepidermal separation is the bedside test for this autoimmune blistering disorder; anti-desmoglein-3 (and 1) antibodies dissolve the desmosomal cadherin adhesions.
essential thrombocythemia: One 2016 WHO revised criteria for this Philadelphia-negative myeloproliferative neoplasm require sustained platelet count above 450,000 plus a driver mutation in JAK2 V617F, CALR, or MPL; hemorrhage and thrombosis compete for the leading morbidity.
spondyloarthropathy: One 1991 European Spondyloarthropathy Study Group set of criteria unified this family of HLA-B27-associated seronegative diseases — ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis, and IBD-associated arthritis — by inflammatory back pain plus enthesitis.
wegener granulomatosis: One 1936 Friedrich Wegener triad from a Breslau autopsy series described this necrotizing vasculitis's classical involvement of upper airway, lung, and kidney; c-ANCA staining directed at PR3 is the serologic footprint.
adult onset stills disease: One 1971 Eric Bywaters description in Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases coined this term for the adult form of a systemic juvenile inflammatory arthritis; salmon-pink evanescent rash, quotidian fevers, and ferritin above 5-fold normal complete the Yamaguchi criteria.
acute intermittent porphyria: One classical port-wine-colored urine on standing signals this autosomal-dominant deficiency of hydroxymethylbilane synthase; abdominal pain, autonomic instability, and psychiatric symptoms follow ingestion of cytochrome-P450-inducing drugs or fasting.
polyarteritis nodosa: One 1866 Kussmaul and Maier autopsy of a young journeyman coined this term for the necrotizing medium-vessel vasculitis studded with palpable nodules along arteries; hepatitis B is the classical associated infection.
acromegaly: One 1886 Pierre Marie coinage in the Revue de Médecine gave this pituitary-somatotroph adenoma disease its Greek-rooted 'extremity enlargement' name; André the Giant and Richard Kiel are its most-recognized modern faces.
myasthenia gravis: One 1934 Mary Walker pyridostigmine bedside challenge established this neuromuscular disorder's diagnosis; anti-acetylcholine-receptor antibodies at the postsynaptic muscle endplate produce the fatigable ptosis and diplopia that worsen through the day.
cushings disease: One 1932 Harvey Cushing description of eight patients with pituitary basophilism at Johns Hopkins launched the modern eponym for this glucocorticoid excess disease; low-dose dexamethasone suppression separates the ACTH-driven pituitary form from ectopic sources.
von hippel lindau: One 1904 Eugen von Hippel retinal hemangioblastoma report and Arvid Lindau's 1927 CNS series established this autosomal-dominant tumor-predisposition syndrome; the responsible tumor-suppressor gene on 3p25 also drives sporadic clear-cell renal cell carcinoma.
complex regional pain syndrome: One 1994 Merskey and Bogduk IASP renaming replaced the older 'reflex sympathetic dystrophy' with this term; the Budapest criteria require sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor, and motor-trophic signs in an extremity after a triggering minor injury.
buerger disease: One 1908 Leo Buerger monograph on this segmental thrombotic vasculitis of small and medium arteries of young smokers described its digital ischemia and shepherd's-crook collaterals on angiography; smoking cessation is the only disease-modifying therapy.
behcet disease: One 1937 Hulusi Behçet report in Dermatologische Wochenschrift described this variable-vessel vasculitis's triad of oral ulcers, genital ulcers, and uveitis; a positive pathergy test to needle stick is characteristic in Silk-Road-belt populations.
stiff person syndrome: One 1956 Frederick Moersch and Henry Woltman Mayo Clinic series described this rare progressive axial-and-limb rigidity syndrome; anti-GAD-65 antibodies against the presynaptic GABA-synthesizing enzyme underlie most cases and Céline Dion put it in the news.
hemochromatosis: One 1976 M.G. Simon HLA-linked pedigree pinned this iron-overload disorder to chromosome 6; the HFE C282Y mutation drives Celtic-population dominance, and the bronzed-diabetes-cirrhosis triad remains the classical late presentation.
lymphangioleiomyomatosis: One 2011 pivotal MILES trial (sirolimus) established mTOR inhibition as disease-modifying for this progressive cystic lung disease of women of childbearing age; abnormal smooth-muscle-like LAM cells proliferate along the pulmonary lymphatics.
mcardle disease: One 1951 Brian McArdle Guy's Hospital description of a young man with exercise-induced cramps and myoglobinuria launched the recognition of this glycogen-storage disorder; myophosphorylase deficiency blocks glycogenolysis and produces the classical 'second wind' phenomenon.
